title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Two: In which Sophie is compelled to seek her fortune (Fragment 1)
text:
Howl‚Äôs Moving Castle

Chapter Two

I N WHICH S OPHIE IS COMPELLED TO SEEK HER FORTUNE

‚ÄúWhat?‚ÄĚ Sophie stared at the girl on the stool opposite her. She looked just like Lettie. She was wearing Lettie‚Äôs second-best blue dress, a wonderful blue that suited her perfectly. She had Lettie‚Äôs dark hair and blue eyes.

‚ÄúI am Martha,‚ÄĚ said her sister. ‚ÄúWho did you catch cutting up Lettie‚Äôs silk drawers? I never told Lettie that. Did you?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúNo,‚ÄĚ said Sophie, quite stunned. She could see it was Martha now. There was Martha‚Äôs tilt to Lettie‚Äôs head, and Martha‚Äôs way of clasping her hands round her knees with her thumbs twiddling. ‚ÄúWhy?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI‚Äôve been dreading you coming to see me,‚ÄĚ Martha said, ‚Äúbecause I knew I‚Äôd have to tell you. It‚Äôs a relief now I have. Promise you won‚Äôt tell anyone. I know you won‚Äôt tell if you promise. You‚Äôre so honorable.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI promise,‚ÄĚ Sophie said. ‚ÄúBut why? How?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúLettie and I arranged it,‚ÄĚ Martha said, twiddling her thumbs, ‚Äúbecause Lettie wanted to learn witchcraft and I didn‚Äôt. Lettie‚Äôs got brains, and she wants a future where she can use them‚ÄĒonly try telling that to Mother! Mother‚Äôs too jealous of Lettie even to admit she has brains!‚ÄĚ

Sophie could not believe Fanny was like that, but she let it pass. ‚ÄúBut what about you?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúEat your cake,‚ÄĚ said Martha. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs good. Oh, yes, I can be clever too. It only took me two weeks at Mrs. Fairfax‚Äôs to find the spell we‚Äôre using. I got up at night and read her books secretly, and it was easy really. Then I asked if I could visit my family and Mrs. Fairfax said yes. She‚Äôs a dear. She thought I was homesick. So I took the spell and came here, and Lettie went back to Mrs. Fairfax pretending to be me. The difficult part was the first week, when I didn‚Äôt know all the things I was supposed to know. It was awful. But I discovered that people like me‚ÄĒthey do, you know, if you like them ‚ÄĒand then it was all right. And Mrs. Fairfax hasn‚Äôt kicked Lettie out, so I suppose she managed too.‚ÄĚ

Sophie chomped at cake she was not really tasting. ‚ÄúBut what made you want to do this?‚ÄĚ

Martha rocked on her stool, grinning all over Lettie‚Äôs face, twirling her thumbs in a happy pink whirl. ‚ÄúI want to get married and have ten children.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúYou‚Äôre not old enough!‚ÄĚ said Sophie.

‚ÄúNot quite,‚ÄĚ Martha agreed. ‚ÄúBut you can see I‚Äôve got to start quite soon in order to fit ten children in. And this way gives me time to wait and see if the person I want likes me for being me. The spell‚Äôs going to wear off gradually, and I shall get more and more like myself, you see.‚ÄĚ

Sophie was so astonished that she finished her cake without noticing what kind it had been. ‚ÄúWhy ten children?‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúBecause that‚Äôs how many I want,‚ÄĚ said Martha.

‚ÄúI never knew!‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúWell, it wasn‚Äôt much good going on about it when you were so busy backing Mother up about me making my fortune,‚ÄĚ Martha said. ‚ÄúYou thought Mother meant it. I did too, until Father died and I saw she was just trying to get rid of us‚ÄĒputting Lettie where she was bound to meet a lot of men and get married off, and sending me as far away as she could! I was so angry I thought, Why not? And I spoke to Lettie and she was just as angry and we fixed it up. We‚Äôre fine now. But we both feel bad about you. You‚Äôre far too clever and nice to be stuck in that shop for the rest of your life. We talked about it, but we couldn‚Äôt see what to do.‚ÄĚ

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Two: In which Sophie is compelled to seek her fortune

vocabulary.definition: A gentle feeling of fondness or liking.
vocabulary.example: I discovered that people like me.

vocabulary.definition: Put into a proper order or made plans for something.
vocabulary.example: Lettie and I arranged it.

vocabulary.definition: Very surprised or amazed.
vocabulary.example: Sophie was so astonished.

vocabulary.definition: Supporting or defending someone or something.
vocabulary.example: You were so busy backing Mother up.

vocabulary.definition: Holding something tightly with the hands.
vocabulary.example: and Martha‚Äôs way of clasping her hands round her knees...

vocabulary.definition: Quick to understand, learn, and devise or apply ideas; intelligent.
vocabulary.example: I can be clever too.

vocabulary.definition: Forced or obliged to do something.
vocabulary.example: I N WHICH S OPHIE IS COMPELLED TO SEEK HER FORTUNE

vocabulary.definition: Ceased to live.
vocabulary.example: Until Father died.

vocabulary.definition: Needing effort or skill to do, deal with, or understand.
vocabulary.example: The difficult part was the first week.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling anxious or fearful about something that is going to happen.
vocabulary.example: I‚Äôve been dreading you coming to see me.

vocabulary.definition: Manage to find time or space for something.
vocabulary.example: I‚Äôve got to start soon in order to fit ten children in.

vocabulary.definition: Chance or luck as an external, arbitrary force affecting one's life.
vocabulary.example: Compelled to seek her fortune.

vocabulary.definition: In a slow manner over a period of time.
vocabulary.example: The spell‚Äôs going to wear off gradually.

vocabulary.definition: Smiling broadly, especially as an expression of amusement or pleasure.
vocabulary.example: Martha rocking on her stool, grinning all over Lettie‚Äôs face.

vocabulary.definition: Deserving respect or high regard; morally good.
vocabulary.example: You‚Äôre so honorable.

vocabulary.definition: Come into the presence or company of someone.
vocabulary.example: Putting Lettie where she was bound to meet a lot of men.

vocabulary.definition: To act as if something is true when it is not.
vocabulary.example: Lettie went back pretending to be me.

vocabulary.definition: Acting as if one is another person or thing.
vocabulary.example: Pretending to be me.

vocabulary.definition: A feeling of reassurance and comfort following distress or anxiety.
vocabulary.example: It‚Äôs a relief now I have.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that is kept hidden from others.
vocabulary.example: I read her books secretly.

vocabulary.definition: Unable to move or unable to change a situation.
vocabulary.example: Far too clever and nice to be stuck in that shop.

vocabulary.definition: So shocked or surprised that one is temporarily unable to react.
vocabulary.example: No,‚ÄĚ said Sophie, quite stunned.

vocabulary.definition: To repeatedly rotate or fiddle with something, often fingers or thumbs.
vocabulary.example: with her thumbs twiddling.

vocabulary.definition: Spinning or rotating rapidly.
vocabulary.example: Twirling her thumbs in a happy pink whirl.

vocabulary.definition: The practice of magical skills, spells, and abilities attributed to witches.
vocabulary.example: Lettie wanted to learn witchcraft.
